primary_sources.title: Experience-weighted Attraction Learning in Normal Form Games
Self-tuning experience weighted attraction learning in games
primary_sources.authors: Teck-Hua Ho; Colin Camerer; Juin-Kuan Chong

formal_model: Attraction update A_i^j(t) = [phi*N(t-1)*A_i^j(t-1) + (delta + (1-delta)*I(j=chosen))*payoff_i^j(t)] / N(t), with experience weight N(t) = rho*N(t-1) + 1; choice via logit P_i^j = exp(lambda*A_i^j)/sum_k exp(lambda*A_i^k). Nests cumulative reinforcement (delta=0, rho=0) and weighted fictitious play / belief learning (delta=1, rho=phi). Parameters: phi (decay of prior attractions), delta (foregone-payoff weight), rho (decay of experience weight), kappa (cumulative vs averaging), lambda (logit precision), N(0) (initial experience weight).
causal_assumptions: Repeated play of a fixed normal-form game with a stationary payoff structure across rounds
Player observes (or can compute) foregone payoffs for unchosen strategies
Attractions are a sufficient statistic for choice; choice is logit in attractions
Parameters are constant over rounds and homogeneous within the estimated population (representative-agent MLE)
No sophisticated/anticipatory reasoning about opponents beyond the belief-learning special case
temporal_scale: event (per round of repeated play; identification needs many rounds)

reported_coefficients.name: delta (foregone-payoff weight)
reported_coefficients.source_ref: Camerer & Ho 1999, Discussion p.868 (and abstract: 'delta generally around .50')
reported_coefficients.context: Per game-class averages of the foregone-payoff weight from MLE calibration; jackknife SEs used in paper but not extracted here

reported_coefficients.name: phi (decay of prior attractions)
reported_coefficients.source_ref: Camerer & Ho 1999 p.869 ('decay parameters phi and rho average ...')
reported_coefficients.context: phi>1 in beauty contests flagged by authors as likely misspecification (adaptive model cannot extrapolate trends)

reported_coefficients.name: rho (decay of experience weight)
reported_coefficients.source_ref: Camerer & Ho 1999 p.869; abstract ('rho varies from 0 to phi')
reported_coefficients.context: rho=0 (median-action) => cumulative reinforcement-like; rho~phi => averaging/belief-like. rho,phi correlated ~0.88 in constant-sum (partial identification)

reported_coefficients.name: N(0) (initial experience weight)
reported_coefficients.context: Large N(0) => slow learning (heavy weight on lagged attractions). N(0),rho not separately identified when declining-effect constraint binds

reported_coefficients.name: lambda (logit precision)
reported_coefficients.context: Estimated jointly per game class; payoff-scale-dependent; specific values not extracted this session

datasets_in_literature: 4x4 and 6x6 constant-sum games in rupees (Mookherjee & Sopher 1997)
Median-action / weak-link order-statistic coordination games (Van Huyck, Battalio & Beil)
p-beauty contest / dominance-solvable games (Ho, Camerer & Weigelt 1998)
replication_evidence: Self-tuning EWA (Ho, Camerer & Chong 2007, JET) reproduces fit with far fewer free parameters, addressing the over-parameterization critique. EWA has been widely re-estimated. However Salmon 2001 (Econometrica 69:1597) shows learning-model discrimination has low power, and Wilcox 2006 (Econometrica 74:1271) shows representative-agent EWA estimates are biased under unmodeled heterogeneity.
critiques_or_nulls: 4-6 free parameters; identification needs many rounds (authors note rho-phi correlation ~0.88 in constant-sum games)
Parameter estimates vary widely across game classes (delta 0.23-0.85; phi 0.80-1.33), so no single transportable parameterization
Salmon 2001: econometric tests have low power to distinguish EWA from reinforcement/belief special cases
Wilcox 2006: representative-agent estimation biased when individual heterogeneity is present
applicability_limits: Repeated normal-form games with cardinal, observable (and foregone) payoffs
Laboratory settings with many rounds and small strategy sets
Not directly applicable to one-shot or field social choices without a repeated-feedback structure
transport_risks: Transport from lab repeated games to field/social decisions unvalidated (high)
local_refit_feasible: Limited. EWA dynamics require repeated-play panels with round-by-round realized AND foregone payoffs. In-repo MobLab/BehaviorBench economic games (dictator, ultimatum, trust, public goods, guessing) are largely one-shot cross-sections, so the (phi,delta,rho,N0) dynamics are not identifiable without a multi-round game panel (a repeated p-beauty/guessing panel would be the only candidate, if round-level data exist).
best_validation_route: Refit MLE on an external repeated-game dataset with feedback (Camerer-Ho / Van Huyck data), hold out later rounds or a left-out game class, and test EWA against its reinforcement (delta=0) and belief (delta=1,rho=phi) special cases via out-of-sample choice-probability MSD.
planned_implementation: Keep EWAState executable; add an MLE fitter for (phi,delta,rho,kappa,lambda,N0) on repeated-game panels; expose the reinforcement and belief special-case restrictions as selectable variants; adopt self-tuning EWA as a low-parameter default.
planned_parameter_packs: ewa_camererho1999_reference: constant_sum(delta0.42/phi1.00/rho0.94/N0 15.80), median_action(delta0.85/phi0.80/rho0/N0 0.65), beauty_contest(delta0.23/phi1.33/rho0.94/N0 16.82) -- labeled lab-reference priors, NOT fit on target domain
promotion_blocker: No local held-out validation record; no repeated-game panel in-repo to identify the learning dynamics; published coefficients are lab-reference priors that vary by game class and are not fit on any target domain.

primary_sources.title: Quantal Response Equilibria for Normal Form Games
primary_sources.authors: Richard D. McKelvey; Thomas R. Palfrey

primary_sources.title: Quantal Response Equilibrium: A Stochastic Theory of Games
primary_sources.authors: Jacob K. Goeree; Charles A. Holt; Thomas R. Palfrey

formal_model: Logit QRE: each player's choice probability P_i(a) = exp(lambda * EU_i(a; P))/sum_b exp(lambda * EU_i(b; P)), a fixed point in which expected utilities are computed against the equilibrium mixed profile. As lambda->0 choices are uniform; as lambda->infinity QRE converges to Nash. The single-agent 'logit choice' reduction takes expected utilities as given (no equilibrium fixed point).
causal_assumptions: Players best-respond stochastically with logit (extreme-value) payoff noise
Beliefs are consistent with the actual mixed profile (equilibrium version) or exogenously supplied (logit-choice reduction)
Payoffs are cardinal and on a common, known scale
A single precision parameter lambda governs all players

reported_coefficients.name: lambda (logit precision)
reported_coefficients.value_or_null: reported ~0.25 to ~4.64 across the experimental datasets McKelvey-Palfrey re-estimated
reported_coefficients.source_ref: McKelvey & Palfrey 1995 (as summarized in secondary literature)
reported_coefficients.context: NOT verified against the 1995 primary table in this session; lambda is payoff-scale-dependent and not comparable across games

reported_coefficients.name: lambda limiting behavior
reported_coefficients.context: Structural limiting property of logit QRE (verified from the model definition)

datasets_in_literature: Normal-form matrix-game experiments re-analyzed by McKelvey-Palfrey (e.g., Ochs 1995 games)
In-repo: BehaviorBench/MobLab games -- lambda_per_game already fitted inside the social_preference_population record (wmv2_behaviorbench_policy.json)
replication_evidence: QRE is one of the most widely re-estimated behavioral-game models; Goeree-Holt-Palfrey 2016 monograph consolidates it. BUT Haile, Hortacsu & Kosenok 2008 (AER 98:180-200) show that logit QRE without restrictions on the error distribution is essentially unfalsifiable -- it can rationalize almost any choice data -- so predictive content depends on imposing the logit form and cross-game lambda restrictions.
critiques_or_nulls: Haile, Hortacsu & Kosenok 2008: unrestricted QRE can fit any data; falsifiability requires the logit/structural assumption
lambda is not comparable across payoff scales; must be refit per game
In-repo, QRE+Fehr-Schmidt (social_preference_population) beats naive baselines but LOSES to a per-game fitted histogram in-distribution (W1 0.099 vs 0.038)
applicability_limits: Requires cardinal payoffs and a defined action set
Equilibrium version assumes belief consistency; better suited to static discrete choice
transport_risks: lambda refit per game / payoff scale (medium-high)
logit error form is a modeling choice that carries the model's falsifiability
local_refit_feasible: Yes. The logit_choice operator is executable and lambda is already fit per game on BehaviorBench inside social_preference_population; logit choice can also be fit as a generic stochastic-choice layer to any in-repo choice-frequency dataset (Upworthy CTR, StackExchange answered).
best_validation_route: Held-out choice-distribution prediction on BehaviorBench per game with lambda refit, benchmarked against the per-game histogram and hand-crafted baselines (note the existing in-distribution failure vs histogram); require payoff-scale normalization before comparing lambda across games.
planned_implementation: Keep logit_choice; add per-game lambda MLE with a payoff-scale normalizer and a falsifiability guard (reject when lambda absorbs arbitrary structure); optionally add a QRE fixed-point solver for the equilibrium version.
planned_parameter_packs: qre_lambda_per_game_behaviorbench: fitted lambda per MobLab game (already in wmv2_behaviorbench_policy.json) -- lab economic games, transport to field unvalidated
promotion_blocker: No PASSED held-out record that beats a strong per-game baseline (current best in-distribution loses to the histogram); lambda not transportable across payoff scales; falsifiability requires imposing scale-normalized cross-game restrictions.

primary_sources.title: Predicting How People Play Games: Reinforcement Learning in Experimental Games with Unique, Mixed Strategy Equilibria
Learning in Extensive-Form Games: Experimental Data and Simple Dynamic Models in the Intermediate Term
Q-learning
primary_sources.authors: Christopher J. C. H. Watkins; Peter Dayan

formal_model: Roth-Erev propensity model: q_nj(t+1) = (1-phi)*q_nj(t) + E_k(j, R(x)), choice P = q_nj / sum_k q_nk, with reinforcement R(x)=payoff - x_min, forgetting/recency parameter phi, and a 'three-step' generalization/experimentation function E_k with parameter epsilon that spreads reinforcement to similar strategies. Basic 1-parameter model has only the initial-propensity strength s(1). A 4-parameter FP-like generalization adds a foregone-info weight d, a maximization parameter m, and a habit/past-experience weight h. Generic value form: Q <- Q + alpha*(r-Q).
causal_assumptions: Choices are reinforced by realized payoff only (law of effect); foregone payoffs ignored in the basic model
Propensities map to probabilities by normalization (relative strength)
Stationary environment; many repetitions with small strategy sets
Recency (forgetting) and local generalization (experimentation) add responsiveness
temporal_scale: event (per round; designed for 100+ round games)

reported_coefficients.name: s(1) basic 1-parameter model (initial-propensity strength)
reported_coefficients.context: Best single value over 12 games; model MSD 1.0 (<30% of equilibrium error); predictive (leave-one-game-out) MSD 1.02; random-choice MSD 1.87

reported_coefficients.name: 3-parameter RE model {s(1), experimentation epsilon, forgetting phi} aggregate fit
reported_coefficients.source_ref: Erev & Roth 1998 p.867 (Roth-Erev 1995 three-parameter RE model)
reported_coefficients.context: Specific fitted epsilon and phi values not extracted this session; aggregate mean-squared-deviation 0.59 when fit simultaneously to all games

reported_coefficients.name: 4-parameter FP-like generalization optimal set {d, m, h, s(1)}
reported_coefficients.context: d=foregone-info weight, m=maximization, h=force of habit; only h (habit) meaningfully improves fit -- the two 'rationality' params (m, FP-expectations) add only ~7% of the 0.16 gain

reported_coefficients.name: generic Q-learning learning rate alpha
reported_coefficients.source_ref: Watkins & Dayan 1992 (algorithm; not a fitted game value)
reported_coefficients.context: alpha in (0,1] is a reference-class parameter, must be fit per environment

datasets_in_literature: 12 experimental games with unique mixed-strategy equilibria (Suppes & Atkinson; Ochs 1995; O'Neill 1987; Rapoport-Boebel; etc.) plus a new Erev-Roth experiment
replication_evidence: Erev-Roth's own out-of-sample prediction (basic predictive MSD 1.02; FP-like 0.84) and very wide subsequent reuse (Bornstein, Nagel, Slonim-Roth, etc.). Salmon 2001 (Econometrica) cautions that learning-model discrimination has low power.
critiques_or_nulls: Basic reinforcement misses late convergence toward equilibrium (fails the observed late 'direction shift')
Only the habit parameter h improves the generalized model; added 'rationality' (maximization, FP beliefs) contributes ~7%
Salmon 2001: low statistical power to distinguish reinforcement from belief/EWA
s(1) and reinforcement scale with payoff magnitude -- not transportable across payoff scales
applicability_limits: Repeated games, many rounds, small strategy sets, stationary environment; laboratory
Generic Q-update is reusable as a value-learning primitive but the fitted game values are lab-specific
transport_risks: s(1)/alpha scale with payoffs and game class (high)
Designed for 100+ round mixed-equilibrium games; transport to field behavior unvalidated
local_refit_feasible: Limited. Requires repeated-play panels with per-round payoffs; in-repo MobLab games are mostly one-shot. OmniBehavior/Kuaishou repeated daily engagement could support a loose 'reinforcement of a binary action' fit, but not the mixed-equilibrium game setting the model was built for.
best_validation_route: Refit the propensity model on an external repeated-game dataset (hold out later rounds / a left-out game), or map the Q-update onto OmniBehavior repeat-engagement and test whether payoff-reinforced propensity beats an AR/momentum baseline held-out.
expected_applicability: narrow for game learning; the Q-update primitive is broadly reusable
planned_implementation: Keep reinforcement_update; add the full Roth-Erev propensity model with {s(1), epsilon, phi} and an MLE fitter; expose forgetting and local-experimentation; keep generic Q-learning alpha as a labeled prior.
planned_parameter_packs: roth_erev1998_reference: basic s(1)=54; 4-param FP-like d0.9/m1.5/h0.1/s1 27 (MSD 0.72) -- labeled lab-reference priors from 12-game calibration
promotion_blocker: No local held-out validation; no repeated-game panel in-repo; published parameters are payoff-scale-dependent lab-reference priors.

primary_sources.title: Individual Learning in Normal Form Games: Some Laboratory Results
The Theory of Learning in Games
primary_sources.venue: MIT Press (companion survey: 'Learning in games', European Economic Review 42:631-639)

formal_model: gamma-weighted beliefs over an opponent's action frequencies: recent observations weighted more, older observations discounted by gamma per period. gamma=0 => Cournot best-response (last period only); gamma=1 => (unweighted) fictitious play. Expected payoffs are computed against these beliefs and mapped to choice via a probabilistic best-response with response sensitivity beta. Registry executable form is a Dirichlet-style count updater.
causal_assumptions: Opponent's play is treated as a stationary (gamma-weighted) distribution
Player best-responds probabilistically to belief-weighted expected payoffs
Recency weight gamma and response sensitivity beta are constant per player
Opponent actions are observable (full or sampled history)

reported_coefficients.name: gamma (belief-decay / recency) -- median across individuals, 3-parameter model
reported_coefficients.context: Medians fall in the lower half of the adaptive (0,1) range, sometimes near the Cournot value 0; the fictitious-play hypothesis gamma=1 is firmly rejected

reported_coefficients.name: beta (response sensitivity / choice precision) -- median
reported_coefficients.source_ref: Cheung & Friedman 1997, Table II; text p.64-65
reported_coefficients.context: 0.42 is retained as the Hawk-Dove benchmark; beta plays the role of logit precision on the belief-based expected payoffs

datasets_in_literature: Cheung-Friedman laboratory normal-form games: Hawk-Dove, Coordination, Battle-of-Sexes, Buyer-Seller, under varying information and matching protocols
replication_evidence: Consistent with Camerer & Ho 1999, in which belief learning is the EWA special case (delta=1, rho=phi) and is usually dominated by EWA (belief models win only in some constant-sum games). Fudenberg-Levine provide the convergence theory.
critiques_or_nulls: EWA (Camerer-Ho 1999) nests belief learning as a restricted case and generally fits better out-of-sample
Individual gamma estimates are noisy: Camerer-Ho note about 1/3 of some parameter estimates are negative and 1/6 above one
Assumes a stationary opponent; fails against adaptive/teaching opponents
applicability_limits: Repeated games with observable opponent actions and a stationary opponent
Laboratory settings; not for one-shot or non-strategic choice
transport_risks: gamma and beta are game- and population-specific (high)
Stationarity assumption breaks with strategic teaching
local_refit_feasible: Limited. Requires repeated games with observable opponent history; in-repo MobLab games are mostly one-shot, so gamma is not identifiable without a repeated-game panel.
best_validation_route: Refit gamma,beta on an external repeated-game panel; test the Cournot (gamma=0), fictitious-play (gamma=1) and EWA nesting via out-of-sample fit.
planned_implementation: Upgrade belief_update_counts to an explicit gamma-weighted (recency-discounted) belief with a logit response beta; expose the Cournot and fictitious-play endpoints as selectable limits.
planned_parameter_packs: cheung_friedman1997_reference: median gamma ~0.26-0.41, beta ~0.42 -- labeled lab-reference priors
promotion_blocker: No local validation; no repeated-game panel in-repo; empirically dominated by EWA where directly compared.

primary_sources.title: How are habits formed: Modelling habit formation in the real world
primary_sources.authors: Phillippa Lally; Cornelia H. M. van Jaarsveld; Henry W. W. Potts; Jane Wardle

primary_sources.title: A new look at habits and the habit-goal interface
Reflections on past behavior: A self-report index of habit strength (SRHI)

formal_model: Automaticity (habit strength) grows with repetition in a stable context following an asymptotic exponential-approach curve toward a plateau; the increment per repetition diminishes as the asymptote is neared. Registry executable form: H <- (1-gamma)*H + gamma*1[acted] (habit-stock accumulation), and habit raises the probability of repeating the behavior (logit boost). Wood & Neal add the context-cue -> response associative account.
causal_assumptions: Repetition of a behavior in a consistent context increases automaticity (context-cue -> behavior association)
Diminishing returns: automaticity approaches an asymptote
A missed single repetition does not materially reset the process
Automaticity, once formed, biases behavior independent of current goals/intentions

reported_coefficients.name: median time to 95% of asymptotic automaticity
reported_coefficients.context: Units = days; from fitting individual asymptotic curves to daily Self-Report Habit Index scores over 84 days; N=96 self-selected behaviors

reported_coefficients.name: N (participants)
reported_coefficients.context: Daily-diary field study; behaviors self-chosen (food/drink/activity)

reported_coefficients.name: per-step accumulation rate gamma (registry parameter)
reported_coefficients.context: Not reported as a single value; strongly behavior- and context-dependent (a labeled prior in the registry)

datasets_in_literature: Lally 2010 UCL daily-diary cohort (SRHI automaticity over 84 days)
Wood, Quinn & Kashy 2002 experience-sampling diary studies (habit in everyday life)
replication_evidence: The 66-day figure is very widely cited and has debunked the '21-day' myth, but it is a single-study median with a wide CI (18-254 days) on self-selected behaviors; it has not been independently replicated at scale. Automaticity is measured by self-report (SRHI), not behavior.
critiques_or_nulls: Automaticity is self-reported (SRHI), not a behavioral measure
Enormous individual variation (18-254 days) and strong dependence on behavior complexity (simple drinking << exercise)
Observational: repetition is not experimentally manipulated, so the asymptote is descriptive not causal
gamma / time-to-automaticity is context-dependent and not transportable as a fixed constant
applicability_limits: Individual behavior repetition in stable contexts
Self-report automaticity domain; not group/strategic behavior
transport_risks: Habit is a robust cross-domain phenomenon but the accumulation rate / plateau time is strongly behavior- and context-dependent (medium)
local_refit_feasible: Partially. OmniBehavior/Kuaishou repeated daily engagement can support a behavioral habit-stock fit (does prior-repetition raise next-period engagement beyond a momentum/AR baseline?). Baby-names data is cultural diffusion, not individual habit. No in-repo dataset has the SRHI automaticity measure.
best_validation_route: Fit habit-stock accumulation on OmniBehavior repeat-engagement and test held-out whether accumulated repetition predicts next-day behavior beyond the engagement_momentum_persistence baseline (which already validated on OmniBehavior).
planned_implementation: Keep habit_update; add a fitter for gamma / time-to-plateau on repeat-behavior panels; explicitly separate self-report automaticity from behavioral repeat-rate; guard against conflating habit with momentum/autocorrelation.
planned_parameter_packs: lally2010_reference: time-to-95%-automaticity median 66 days (range 18-254) -- a reference prior for automaticity growth, NOT a behavioral repeat-rate
promotion_blocker: No local held-out validation; the registry gamma is a labeled prior not fit on target data; primary evidence is self-report and correlational; must be disentangled from the already-validated momentum/persistence family.

primary_sources.title: A Behavioral Model of Rational Choice
Search and Satisficing
primary_sources.authors: Andrew Caplin; Mark Dean; Daniel Martin

formal_model: Sequential search with a reservation utility u*: the agent examines options in sequence, accumulating a consideration set, and STOPS as soon as an option with utility >= u* is encountered (Caplin-Dean-Martin 'Condition 1'). The reservation level is fixed within a standard-choice environment and responds to the environment (rising with set size and object complexity). Simon (1955) is the origin: choose the first option that meets an aspiration level.
causal_assumptions: Agents search sequentially and can be observed switching between provisional choices (choice-process data)
Stopping is governed by a reservation/aspiration threshold on utility
Utility is revealed by incentivized payoffs
Reservation level is fixed within an environment (a declining level is optimal for choice-process settings but is NOT observed empirically)
required_variables: ordered sequence of options considered (choice process)

reported_coefficients.name: consistency with sequential search (Houtman-Maks index)
reported_coefficients.context: HM index = largest fraction of a subject's observations consistent with reservation-based sequential search; N=76 subjects

reported_coefficients.name: failure to select the best option
reported_coefficients.context: Average money left on table $3.12 (17% of max per round); $7.12 in the hardest treatment -- evidence of incomplete/bounded search

reported_coefficients.name: reservation utility level
reported_coefficients.context: Not a single scalar; estimated per treatment and shown to INCREASE with set size and object complexity

reported_coefficients.name: sample size
reported_coefficients.context: Incentivized search experiment with a select-and-update choice-process interface

datasets_in_literature: Caplin-Dean-Martin incentivized sequential-search experiment (choice-process data recording which option is provisionally selected over time)
replication_evidence: Consistent with Reutskaja, Nagel, Camerer & Rangel 2011 (AER) eye-tracking evidence of satisficing/bounded search in consumer choice, and with the York 'When and How to Satisfice' experiments; part of a broad, converging bounded-rationality literature.
critiques_or_nulls: Reservation levels can only be identified with choice-process data (what was examined, in what order) -- rarely available in field data
A declining reservation level is optimal in choice-process settings but is NOT observed (subjects use a fixed, sub-optimal reservation)
Satisficing is one of several competing bounded-search / consideration-set models
transport_risks: Reservation levels are environment-specific (rise with set size/complexity) (medium)
Requires observing the search process, not just the final choice
local_refit_feasible: No. In-repo datasets record final choices/outcomes but NOT the choice process (which options were examined, in what order, with provisional switches). Reservation utilities and search order are therefore not identifiable from BehaviorBench, Upworthy, StackExchange, CMV, or OmniBehavior.
best_validation_route: Cannot be validated on current in-repo data. Requires a choice-process/search dataset (eye-tracking, or click-stream logs of option examination by a single decider). Would first need a new operator + such a dataset.
expected_applicability: medium (consideration-set / bounded search is common) but data-hungry
planned_implementation: NEW family: a reservation-threshold sequential-search operator -- given an ordered option stream and reservation u*, stop at the first option >= u*; expose u* as a fitted/prior consideration-set gate placed before the choice operator; add executable code + test to reach 'implemented'.
planned_parameter_packs: caplin_dean_martin2011_reference (sanity target only, not fittable locally): HM>0.95 for >40% of subjects; ~38% best-option miss rate; reservation rises with set size/complexity
promotion_blocker: Not implemented (no executable code or test); no local choice-process data to identify reservation utility; requires both a new operator and a search-process dataset before any validation is possible.

primary_sources.title: Identifying Social Norms Using Coordination Games: Why Does Dictator Game Sharing Vary?
primary_sources.authors: Erin L. Krupka; Roberto A. Weber
primary_sources.venue: Journal of the European Economic Association 11(3):495-524

primary_sources.title: The Grammar of Society: The Nature and Dynamics of Social Norms

formal_model: Utility U_i(a) = V(pi_i(a)) + gamma_i * N(a), where pi_i(a) is the monetary payoff of action a, N(a) in [-1,+1] is the elicited social-appropriateness rating of a (incentive-compatibly elicited via a coordination game), and gamma_i >= 0 is heterogeneous norm-sensitivity. Choice maximizes (or is logit over) U. Appropriateness is elicited on a 4-point scale mapped to {-1 very inappropriate, -1/3 somewhat inappropriate, +1/3 somewhat appropriate, +1 very appropriate}.
causal_assumptions: Behavior is driven by money payoff PLUS concern for the injunctive norm (elicited appropriateness)
Elicited appropriateness ratings capture common-knowledge social consensus (coordination-game incentive)
gamma_i (norm-sensitivity) is heterogeneous across agents; V() is common
Framing that shifts appropriateness (e.g., 'bully'/take vs standard/give) causally shifts behavior via N(a)
required_variables: elicited appropriateness ratings N(a) per action and context

reported_coefficients.name: social-appropriateness rating scale N(a)
reported_coefficients.context: 4-point evaluation mapped to [-1,+1]; ratings are the elicited norm inputs to the utility model

reported_coefficients.name: example elicited ratings (standard dictator game)
reported_coefficients.context: In the standard DG the equal split is rated appropriate (+0.90) and keeping $9 inappropriate (-0.71); the 1.61 gap drives the predicted preference for sharing

reported_coefficients.name: behavioral prediction test (frame manipulation)
reported_coefficients.value_or_null: predicted behavior shifts across treatments confirmed
reported_coefficients.context: More agents choose the equal split in the 'bully'/take frame than the standard/give frame, as predicted by the norm+money model

reported_coefficients.name: fitted gamma (norm-sensitivity coefficient)
reported_coefficients.context: NOT point-identified in the original paper (gamma modeled as heterogeneous; predictions are directional). Later work (e.g., Kimbrough & Vostroknutov 2016) fits a norm-sensitivity coefficient

datasets_in_literature: Krupka-Weber dictator-game variants (standard, bully/take, and others) plus reanalysis of prior DG studies (List 2007; Bardsley 2008); norms elicited via a coordination game
replication_evidence: The Krupka-Weber elicitation method is very widely replicated and yields robust, incentive-compatible norm ratings that predict behavior across many domains (Kimbrough-Vostroknutov 2016 fit a structural norm-sensitivity; 'When in Rome' and many follow-ups). But a single structural gamma is context-specific.
critiques_or_nulls: Elicited norm ratings are frame/context-specific (bully vs standard differ substantially) -- they must be re-elicited per setting
Coordination-game elicitation can conflate the injunctive norm with focal-point salience
The original paper gives directional predictions; gamma is not point-identified without additional structure
applicability_limits: Requires elicited appropriateness ratings for the specific action set
transport_risks: Norm ratings and gamma are population- and context-specific; cannot transport ratings across frames/cultures without re-elicitation (high)
local_refit_feasible: No for the identified model. In-repo BehaviorBench dictator/ultimatum games record choices but NOT the elicited appropriateness ratings N(a) that the model requires as inputs; a norm-elicitation experiment would be needed. A reduced-form norm proxy could be fit but would not identify the Krupka-Weber model.
best_validation_route: Pair BehaviorBench allocation choices with a fresh elicited norm-rating table and test whether V(pi)+gamma*N(a) predicts choice shifts across variants held-out; without elicited norms the model is not identifiable.
expected_applicability: medium-high (norms pervade social behavior) but requires elicited-norm inputs
planned_implementation: Upgrade norm_compliance from the generic relationship_modulator stand-in to the Krupka-Weber utility U=V(pi)+gamma*N(a); require an elicited norm-rating table as input; expose gamma as fitted/prior; add logit choice over U.
planned_parameter_packs: krupka_weber2013_reference: standard-DG appropriateness ratings (equal-split +0.90, keep-$9 -0.71; scale [-1,+1]) as a reference norm table for allocation frames; gamma as a labeled prior needing per-setting fit
promotion_blocker: No elicited-norm inputs in local data; no local validation record; gamma not point-identified without new elicitation; the current registry operator is a generic modulator, not the identified Krupka-Weber norm model.
